news_summary: Advancements in solid-state battery technology, AI integration in scientific research, precise protein synthesis in octopuses, and breakthroughs in rotational super-radiance highlight today's exciting progress in technology and science.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans le domaine des batteries à état solide, l'intégration de l'intelligence artificielle dans la recherche scientifique, la synthèse précise des protéines chez les pieuvres et les percées en matière de super-radiance rotationnelle illustrent les progrès passionnants réalisés aujourd'hui dans les domaines de la technologie et de la science.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la tecnología de las baterías de estado sólido, la integración de la inteligencia artificial en la investigación científica, la síntesis precisa de proteínas en los pulpos y los avances en la superradiancia rotacional ponen de relieve los emocionantes progresos actuales en el ámbito de la tecnología y la ciencia.

articles.title: The biggest problem with solid-state batteries may finally be solved
articles.summary: Researchers solved the mystery of how soft lithium dendrites crack the hard ceramic inside solid-state batteries, triggering short circuits. The breakthrough could help engineers build safer, longer-lasting batteries for smartphones, electric vehicles, and other electronics.
articles.content:
Smartphones, electric vehicles, and countless portable electronics all depend on batteries. As demand for better energy storage grows, improvements in battery capacity, lifespan, and safety will play a major role in the future of electrification. One of the most promising technologies is the solid-state battery, which could allow smartphones to operate for several days on a single charge and give electric vehicles driving ranges up to three times greater than many current models.

Unlike conventional lithium-ion batteries, which use a liquid electrolyte between two solid electrodes, solid-state batteries replace the liquid with a solid electrolyte. This design offers several potential advantages, including higher energy density, improved safety, and longer battery life. But one stubborn problem has slowed commercial adoption. During charging, tiny tree-like structures called dendrites can grow from the lithium anode, pierce the solid electrolyte, and create internal short circuits.

Now, an interdisciplinary team at the Max Planck Institute for Sustainable Materials (MPI-SusMat) has identified exactly how these dendrites trigger fractures that ultimately lead to battery failure. Their findings were published in the journal Nature.

How Dendrites Crack Solid-State Batteries

Exactly how soft lithium dendrites manage to break through a hard ceramic electrolyte has long puzzled researchers.

"Although the electrodes and the forming dendrites consist of lithium metal, which is soft like a gummy bear, the dendrites are able to penetrate the ceramic electrolyte and lead to a short circuit," says Dr. Yuwei Zhang, first author of the new publication and head of the group "Chemo-Mechanics of Battery Materials" at MPI-SusMat. "How can soft dendrites fracture the stiff solid ceramic? There are two hypotheses: either internal stress is built up inside the dendrites and induces mechanical fracture of the solid electrolyte. Or, electrons leak along the grain boundaries of the solid electrolyte promoting the formation of lithium nuclei that interconnect later."

To determine which explanation was correct, the researchers used an advanced combination of sample preparation and materials characterization techniques. Every step was performed under vacuum and at cryogenic temperatures to eliminate interference from oxygen, water, or even the microscopes' electron beams.

The team examined both the internal stress and the plastic deformation of lithium dendrites trapped inside cracks. Their analysis found no buildup of lithium ahead of the dendrite tip, ruling out one proposed mechanism.

"The soft lithium metal is able to penetrate the stiff ceramic electrolyte, like a continuous waterjet that penetrates a rock. We calculated that hydrostatic stress in the dendrite leads to brittle fracture of the solid electrolyte in the end," says Zhang.

The researchers also confirmed their conclusions using phase field simulations and electron backscatter diffraction measurements.

New Strategies to Prevent Battery Failure

With a better understanding of how dendrites fracture solid electrolytes, the team is now investigating ways to stop or delay the process.

Potential solutions include making the solid electrolyte tougher so it resists cracking for longer, introducing microscopic voids that redirect dendrite growth and steer cracks away from vulnerable areas, or adding protective coatings to lithium electrodes to reduce dendrite formation in the first place.

The researchers say their work demonstrates the importance of understanding how materials behave at the microscopic level. Those insights could help transform solid-state batteries from a promising concept into a practical technology for future smartphones, electric vehicles, and other electronic devices.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough solving a key problem in solid-state batteries, which could lead to safer, longer-lasting batteries with broad applications in smartphones, electric vehicles, and electronics. This advancement has meaningful real-world impact and is well-detailed, focusing on a single important topic with potential large-scale benefits for society and the environment through improved energy storage technology.
articles.personality_title: Scientists solve key problem that caused solid-state batteries to fail
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Solid-state batteries are a new type of battery that use a solid material instead of liquid inside. They could make smartphones last days on one charge and help electric cars drive much farther. But a big problem has stopped them from being used widely: tiny structures called dendrites grow inside and break the solid parts, causing the battery to fail.

**What happened** – Researchers at the Max Planck Institute studied how these soft lithium dendrites manage to crack the hard ceramic inside the batteries. They found that pressure from the dendrites acts like a strong water jet that breaks the ceramic. This discovery was made using special tools that keep the samples very cold and free from air, allowing a close look at the materials inside.

**Impact** – Understanding exactly how dendrites cause cracks is important because it helps scientists find ways to stop or slow this damage. This could lead to making solid-state batteries safer and longer-lasting. Such batteries could improve the performance and safety of many devices, from phones to electric cars.

**What’s next step** – The research team is now exploring new ideas like making the solid parts tougher, creating tiny spaces to redirect dendrites, or adding coatings to stop dendrites from forming. These strategies aim to make solid-state batteries practical for everyday use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have uncovered how soft lithium dendrites break solid electrolytes, paving the way for safer, longer-lasting solid-state batteries in future electronics.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques résolvent un problème clé des batteries à état solide
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les batteries à état solide sont un nouveau type de batterie qui utilisent un matériau solide au lieu d’un liquide à l’intérieur. Elles pourraient permettre aux smartphones de fonctionner plusieurs jours avec une seule charge et aux voitures électriques de parcourir bien plus de distance. Mais un problème majeur a freiné leur utilisation : de petites structures appelées dendrites poussent à l’intérieur et cassent les parties solides, provoquant la panne de la batterie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Institut Max Planck ont étudié comment ces dendrites de lithium, qui sont molles, parviennent à fissurer la céramique dure à l’intérieur des batteries. Ils ont découvert que la pression exercée par les dendrites agit comme un jet d’eau puissant qui casse la céramique. Cette découverte a été possible grâce à des outils spéciaux qui maintiennent les échantillons très froids et sans air, permettant d’examiner de près les matériaux.

**Impact** – Comprendre précisément comment les dendrites provoquent des fissures est important car cela aide les scientifiques à trouver des moyens d’arrêter ou de ralentir ces dégâts. Cela pourrait rendre les batteries à état solide plus sûres et plus durables. Ces batteries pourraient améliorer les performances et la sécurité de nombreux appareils, des téléphones aux voitures électriques.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche explore maintenant de nouvelles idées comme rendre les parties solides plus résistantes, créer de petits espaces pour détourner les dendrites, ou ajouter des revêtements pour empêcher leur formation. Ces stratégies visent à rendre les batteries à état solide pratiques pour un usage quotidien.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert comment les dendrites de lithium cassent les électrolytes solides, ouvrant la voie à des batteries à état solide plus sûres et plus durables pour les futurs appareils électroniques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos resuelven problema clave que causaba fallas en baterías de estado sólido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las baterías de estado sólido son un tipo nuevo de batería que usa un material sólido en lugar de líquido en su interior. Podrían hacer que los teléfonos funcionen varios días con una sola carga y que los autos eléctricos recorran mucho más. Pero un gran problema ha detenido su uso: pequeñas estructuras llamadas dendritas crecen dentro y rompen las partes sólidas, haciendo que la batería falle.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Instituto Max Planck estudiaron cómo estas dendritas de litio suaves logran agrietar la cerámica dura dentro de las baterías. Descubrieron que la presión de las dendritas actúa como un chorro de agua fuerte que rompe la cerámica. Este hallazgo se logró usando herramientas especiales que mantienen las muestras muy frías y sin aire, permitiendo observar de cerca los materiales.

**Impacto** – Entender exactamente cómo las dendritas causan grietas es importante porque ayuda a los científicos a encontrar formas de detener o retrasar este daño. Esto podría hacer que las baterías de estado sólido sean más seguras y duraderas. Estas baterías podrían mejorar el rendimiento y la seguridad de muchos dispositivos, desde teléfonos hasta autos eléctricos.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación ahora explora nuevas ideas como hacer las partes sólidas más resistentes, crear espacios diminutos para redirigir las dendritas, o añadir recubrimientos para evitar que se formen. Estas estrategias buscan hacer que las baterías de estado sólido sean prácticas para el uso diario.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos descubrieron cómo las dendritas de litio rompen los electrolitos sólidos, abriendo el camino para baterías de estado sólido más seguras y duraderas en futuros dispositivos electrónicos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate, translucent crystal lattice being gently but steadily penetrated by soft, glowing tendrils resembling tiny branching roots, set against a simple, natural background that symbolizes strength and resilience, with subtle protective shields forming around the lattice to represent new strategies preventing fractures.

articles.title: Which ‘AI scientist’ suits your lab? A guide for the perplexed
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 10 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02091-6General purpose AI tools for science, such as Claude Science, promise to accelerate research. But which is right for you?
articles.content:
Anthropic released Claude Science in June. It joins a host of other artificial-intelligence tools for researchers.Credit: Blossom Stock Studio/Shutterstock

In 2010, Euan Ashley, a geneticist and cardiologist at Stanford University in California, led the first clinical analysis of a human genome, which took his team of 31 scientists nine months to complete1.

This week, while unpacking after a holiday, Ashley asked the AI tool Claude, developed by Anthropic in San Francisco, California, to examine his own genome to the same standard.

The analysis took 30 minutes and correctly identified an Alzheimer’s disease risk allele and gene variants affecting drug metabolism (Ashley had analysed his genome in 2012 but did not publish the results). “There is no world in which this is not utterly remarkable,” Ashley wrote in a LinkedIn post.

On 30 June, Anthropic unveiled a platform called Claude Science, designed with biology research firmly in mind. The tool joins a department’s worth of general purpose AI tools for science created by technology firms and academic laboratories. Others include offerings from OpenAI in San Francisco and Co-Scientist from Google DeepMind in Mountain View, California. Another is an open-source tool called Biomni, developed by academic researchers and described yesterday in Science2. And there are many others, researchers say.

“Work that usually takes me hours now takes minutes. I can really spend my time on the science that needs a human,” says co-author Yuanhao Qu, the co-founder and president of Phylo, a start-up firm in South San Francisco, California.

What are these and how are scientists using them?

Sometimes called 'AI scientists', these tools are based on the large language models that power chatbots, helping scientists with tasks such as literature reviews, data analysis, figure generation and manuscript preparation. They are a form of agentic AI, in which requests are broken down into steps that often involve recruiting external software systems.

These scientific agents are distinct from more specialized research tools, such as the AlphaFold protein-structure-prediction model, but they can employ bespoke models. For example, Gabriele Corso, co-founder and chief executive of the London-based firm Boltz, and his team tasked a Claude agent to design an antibody that recognized two therapeutic targets, using the company’s open-source AI tools for protein-folding prediction and design.

AI ‘scientists’ joined these research teams: here’s what happened

The AI’s outputs aligned with the protein designers’ intuitions; Corso's team did not validate the designs experimentally, but other antibodies made with AI agents have been, he says. Boltz’s tools are among the dozens of specialized software systems that Claude Science and other AI scientists can interact with.

Clare Bryant, an immunologist at the University of Cambridge, UK, was an early adopter of Co-Scientist, which mines the scientific literature and other sources to come up with scientific hypotheses. Bryant, who was investigating immune responses to zoonotic pathogens, provided the tool with a grant application and further data.

Some of the ideas that it generated weren’t doable, but others were right in her lab’s wheelhouse. Her team is now testing an idea from Co-Scientist, introducing specific mutations into an innate-immune protein and seeing how they impact influenza infection. Bryant says she might have eventually come up with the experiment on her own, but it could have taken two years.

“You feel like you’re talking to an oracle,” says Gary Peltz, a biomedical scientist at Stanford, who used Co-Scientist to identify existing drugs that could treat an organoid model of a disease called liver fibrosis3.

How should scientists decide which tools to use?

Many scientists already use AI tools such as Claude to generate presentation slides and draft e-mails. But Ashu Singhal, president and co-founder of the cloud platform Benchling in San Francisco, estimates that less than 20% of labs have fully embedded AI scientists into their research. “It’s really important that people actually try these things out, rather than simply trusting what gets shared in headlines,” he says.

Singhal recommends that researchers trial several tools to work out which ones are suitable for which tasks. Hypothesis-generating AIs, such as Co-Scientist, might help during the earliest stages of a project. Later on, tools such as Claude Science and Biomni could carry out specific tasks, such as genomic data analysis.

Corso recommends that researchers start with small tasks, the output of which can be verified easily. “Worst case, you have to do them over,” he says.

How can researchers trust 'AI scientists'?
articles.reasoning: The article describes the significant positive impact of AI tools like Claude Science on accelerating scientific research, including genome analysis and hypothesis generation, which can broadly benefit the scientific community and society by speeding up discoveries and improving research efficiency. It provides detailed examples and expert opinions, showing substance and meaningful scope beyond niche or promotional content.
articles.personality_title: New AI tools like Claude Science speed up important scientific research
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists often spend a long time analyzing complex data, such as human genomes, to make important discoveries. In the past, these tasks could take months or years. Recently, new artificial intelligence (AI) tools have been created to help scientists work faster and more efficiently.

**What happened** – In June 2026, a company called Anthropic released Claude Science, an AI tool designed specifically for biology research. For example, a scientist named Euan Ashley used Claude to analyze his own genome. What took his team nine months in 2010 was done by Claude in just 30 minutes, correctly finding important genetic information. Other AI tools, like Co-Scientist from Google DeepMind and Biomni from academic researchers, are also helping scientists generate ideas, analyze data, and prepare research papers.

**Impact** – These AI tools are changing how research is done by saving scientists many hours of work. They can quickly review scientific papers, suggest new experiments, and help design important medical treatments like antibodies. For instance, Clare Bryant used Co-Scientist to develop new ideas for fighting flu infections, speeding up research that might have taken years. This allows scientists to focus more on experiments that need human creativity and judgment.

**What's next step** – Researchers are encouraged to try different AI tools to find out which ones help them best. Experts suggest starting with small tasks that are easy to check, so scientists can trust the AI’s results. As more labs use these tools, AI could become a regular part of how scientific discoveries are made.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New AI tools like Claude Science are helping scientists analyze data and generate ideas much faster, making research quicker and more efficient than ever before.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux outils d’IA comme Claude Science accélèrent la recherche scientifique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques passent souvent beaucoup de temps à analyser des données complexes, comme les génomes humains, pour faire des découvertes importantes. Autrefois, ces tâches pouvaient prendre des mois, voire des années. Récemment, de nouveaux outils d’intelligence artificielle (IA) ont été créés pour aider les chercheurs à travailler plus vite et plus efficacement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En juin 2026, une entreprise appelée Anthropic a lancé Claude Science, un outil d’IA conçu spécialement pour la recherche en biologie. Par exemple, un scientifique nommé Euan Ashley a utilisé Claude pour analyser son propre génome. Ce qui avait pris neuf mois à son équipe en 2010 a été fait en seulement 30 minutes par Claude, qui a correctement identifié des informations génétiques importantes. D’autres outils d’IA, comme Co-Scientist de Google DeepMind et Biomni développés par des chercheurs universitaires, aident aussi les scientifiques à générer des idées, analyser des données et préparer des articles.

**Impact** – Ces outils d’IA changent la façon dont la recherche est menée en faisant gagner beaucoup de temps aux scientifiques. Ils peuvent rapidement passer en revue des articles scientifiques, suggérer de nouvelles expériences et aider à concevoir des traitements médicaux importants comme des anticorps. Par exemple, Clare Bryant a utilisé Co-Scientist pour développer de nouvelles idées pour lutter contre la grippe, accélérant des recherches qui auraient pu prendre des années. Cela permet aux chercheurs de se concentrer davantage sur les expériences nécessitant la créativité et le jugement humains.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs sont encouragés à essayer différents outils d’IA pour trouver ceux qui les aident le mieux. Les experts recommandent de commencer par de petites tâches faciles à vérifier afin que les scientifiques puissent avoir confiance dans les résultats de l’IA. À mesure que davantage de laboratoires utiliseront ces outils, l’IA pourrait devenir une partie régulière du processus de découverte scientifique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouveaux outils d’IA comme Claude Science aident les scientifiques à analyser les données et générer des idées beaucoup plus rapidement, rendant la recherche plus rapide et plus efficace que jamais.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas herramientas de IA como Claude Science aceleran la investigación científica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos a menudo dedican mucho tiempo a analizar datos complejos, como genomas humanos, para hacer descubrimientos importantes. Antes, estas tareas podían tomar meses o años. Recientemente, se han creado nuevas herramientas de inteligencia artificial (IA) para ayudar a los investigadores a trabajar más rápido y con mayor eficiencia.

**Qué pasó** – En junio de 2026, una empresa llamada Anthropic lanzó Claude Science, una herramienta de IA diseñada especialmente para la investigación en biología. Por ejemplo, un científico llamado Euan Ashley usó Claude para analizar su propio genoma. Lo que le tomó a su equipo nueve meses en 2010, Claude lo hizo en solo 30 minutos, identificando correctamente información genética importante. Otras herramientas de IA, como Co-Scientist de Google DeepMind y Biomni desarrollada por investigadores académicos, también ayudan a los científicos a generar ideas, analizar datos y preparar artículos.

**Impacto** – Estas herramientas de IA están cambiando la forma en que se hace la investigación al ahorrar muchas horas de trabajo a los científicos. Pueden revisar rápidamente artículos científicos, sugerir nuevos experimentos y ayudar a diseñar tratamientos médicos importantes como anticuerpos. Por ejemplo, Clare Bryant usó Co-Scientist para desarrollar nuevas ideas para combatir la gripe, acelerando una investigación que podría haber tomado años. Esto permite que los científicos se concentren más en experimentos que requieren creatividad y juicio humanos.

**Próximo paso** – Se anima a los investigadores a probar diferentes herramientas de IA para descubrir cuáles les ayudan más. Los expertos sugieren comenzar con tareas pequeñas que sean fáciles de verificar, para que los científicos puedan confiar en los resultados de la IA. A medida que más laboratorios usen estas herramientas, la IA podría convertirse en una parte habitual del proceso de descubrimiento científico.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Nuevas herramientas de IA como Claude Science están ayudando a los científicos a analizar datos y generar ideas mucho más rápido, haciendo que la investigación sea más rápida y eficiente que nunca.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an abstract laboratory scene where glowing, interconnected strands of DNA gently float above an open book of scientific data, surrounded by softly illuminated, stylized AI circuit patterns and delicate molecular models, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Daily briefing: Mutation lets octopuses make proteins with precision
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 09 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02177-1A break in these octopuses’ protein-making machinery boosts their accuracy. Plus, a Nobel-prizewinner has left the United States for China and the effects of microgravity on the brain.
articles.content:
A break in these octopuses’ protein-making machinery boosts their accuracy. Plus, a Nobel-prizewinner has left the United States for China and the effects of microgravity on the brain.

Hello Nature readers, would you like to get this Briefing in your inbox free every day? Sign up here.

Shallow-water octopuses seem to have picked up their protein-building mutation around 100 million years ago, around the same time they developed bigger brains.Credit: Norbert Wu/Minden Pictures via Alamy

Certain species of shallow-water octopuses have a unique mutation that makes them extremely accurate protein builders. Researchers found that this mutation causes a break in an RNA strand in the core of the octopus's protein-making machinery. When they engineered a similar break into Escherichia coli, the bacteria made around 50% fewer mistakes when building proteins than did bacteria without the mutation. This quirk means that proteins in these octopuses are less likely to misfold and form toxic clumps.

Science | 5 min read

Reference: bioRxiv preprint (not peer reviewed)

Nobel-prize-winning chemist Omar Yaghi has left the United States for a full-time position at Tsinghua University in Beijing, where he will lead a new artificial-intelligence-assisted materials discovery institute. Born in Jordan to Palestinian-refugee parents, Yaghi came to the US as a child and did his Nobel-winning work on metal-organic framework compounds there. The current state of US science is “not so encouraging”, Yaghi said recently, “because of the cutting back on grants”. In light of these cuts by the administration of President Donald Trump, China has been trying to lure US talent with the promise of money and support.

Nature | 6 min read

For the first time, geophysicists have caught the ocean floor splitting apart at its seams. Using an array of measuring stations laid across an 100-kilometre-long region of the Indian Ocean, the team witnessed a seismic event that shifted two sections of the oceanic crust apart by at least 2 metres in a matter of days, spewing around 160 million cubic metres of lava onto the sea floor as it did so.

Nature | 4 min read

Reference: Nature paper

Features & opinion

The effects of living in microgravity on our bodies are well documented. The impact of spaceflight on the brain is more of a mystery, and finding out is crucial to knowing whether long-haul space missions are feasible. Now, researchers have pulled together data from studies of astronauts and spaceflight simulation experiments to glean how the absence of gravity physically affects the human brain. “It's a beautiful neuroplasticity — we found that there are both structural and functional alterations,” says cognitive neuroscientist and study co-author Elisa Raffaella Ferrè.

BBC Future | 7 min read

Reference: Frontiers in Psychology paper

In an attempt to pin down the je ne sais quoi that defines life, some scientists have suggested that living things have ‘agency’, that being an ability to pursue a goal. But the term is contentious in biology, not least because there’s no agreed upon definition of agency, either. Some researchers argue that goal-directed behaviour is often just a response to a stimulus, with little or no active choice involved. Others suggest that genes can only provide so many instructions, and that at some point, the organism must take the wheel.

Quanta | 12 min read

Over the past decade, biologists Mathilde Poyet and Mathieu Groussin have been travelling the world to collect microbiome samples from as diverse a range of people as possible. On their travels, the pair also interviewed donors about factors such as diet and lifestyle that might impact the bacteria that live on and in their bodies. Their data — housed in the Global Microbiome Conservancy, one of the biggest repositories of live bacteria in the world — have revealed that there’s no such thing as a ‘normal’ microbiome.

The New York Times Magazine | 20 min read
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about a unique mutation in shallow-water octopuses that improves protein-building accuracy, which has broad implications for biology and biotechnology. The engineered mutation in bacteria reducing protein synthesis errors by 50% shows tangible potential for improving protein production technologies. The story is focused on this breakthrough with sufficient scientific context and impact.
articles.personality_title: Octopus mutation improves protein-building accuracy by 50%
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Proteins are essential molecules in all living things, made by cellular machines that sometimes make mistakes. These errors can cause proteins to fold wrongly and become harmful. Scientists study how different creatures build proteins to understand and improve this process.

**What happened** – Researchers discovered that certain shallow-water octopuses have a special mutation in their protein-making machinery. This mutation breaks a part of their RNA, making their protein-building much more precise. When scientists copied this mutation into bacteria called Escherichia coli, the bacteria made 50% fewer mistakes while building proteins.

**Impact** – This mutation helps octopuses create proteins that are less likely to misfold and cause damage. The finding is important because it shows a natural way to improve protein accuracy, which could help in medicine and biotechnology. For example, it might lead to better ways to produce proteins used in drugs.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely explore how to use this mutation or similar changes in other organisms to improve protein production. They might also study how this mutation evolved and if it can be applied to human medicine or industry.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A unique mutation in shallow-water octopuses boosts protein-building accuracy, and copying it into bacteria reduces errors by half, offering new paths to improve protein production.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une mutation chez les pieuvres améliore de 50 % la précision de fabrication des protéines
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les protéines sont des molécules essentielles à tous les êtres vivants, fabriquées par des machines cellulaires qui font parfois des erreurs. Ces erreurs peuvent entraîner un mauvais pliage des protéines, les rendant nocives. Les scientifiques étudient comment différents animaux fabriquent leurs protéines pour mieux comprendre et améliorer ce processus.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert que certaines pieuvres d’eau peu profonde ont une mutation spéciale dans leur machinerie de fabrication des protéines. Cette mutation casse une partie de leur ARN, ce qui rend la fabrication des protéines beaucoup plus précise. En reproduisant cette mutation chez une bactérie appelée Escherichia coli, les scientifiques ont réduit de 50 % les erreurs de fabrication des protéines.

**Impact** – Cette mutation aide les pieuvres à créer des protéines moins susceptibles de mal se plier et de causer des dommages. Cette découverte est importante car elle montre un moyen naturel d’améliorer la précision dans la fabrication des protéines, ce qui pourrait être utile en médecine et en biotechnologie, par exemple pour produire de meilleurs médicaments.

**Étape suivante** – Les scientifiques vont probablement explorer comment utiliser cette mutation ou des modifications similaires dans d’autres organismes pour améliorer la production de protéines. Ils pourraient aussi étudier l’évolution de cette mutation et ses applications possibles en médecine humaine ou dans l’industrie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une mutation unique chez certaines pieuvres améliore la précision de fabrication des protéines, et la copier chez des bactéries réduit leurs erreurs de moitié, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour améliorer la production de protéines.
articles.personality_title_es: Una mutación en pulpos mejora en un 50 % la precisión en la fabricación de proteínas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las proteínas son moléculas esenciales para todos los seres vivos, fabricadas por máquinas celulares que a veces cometen errores. Estos errores pueden causar que las proteínas se plieguen mal y sean dañinas. Los científicos estudian cómo diferentes animales fabrican proteínas para entender mejor y mejorar este proceso.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores descubrieron que ciertos pulpos de aguas poco profundas tienen una mutación especial en su maquinaria para fabricar proteínas. Esta mutación rompe una parte de su ARN, haciendo que la fabricación de proteínas sea mucho más precisa. Cuando copiaron esta mutación en una bacteria llamada Escherichia coli, las bacterias cometieron un 50 % menos de errores al fabricar proteínas.

**Impacto** – Esta mutación ayuda a los pulpos a crear proteínas que tienen menos probabilidades de plegarse mal y causar daño. El hallazgo es importante porque muestra una forma natural de mejorar la precisión en la fabricación de proteínas, lo que podría ayudar en medicina y biotecnología, por ejemplo, para producir mejores medicamentos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente explorarán cómo usar esta mutación o cambios similares en otros organismos para mejorar la producción de proteínas. También podrían estudiar cómo evolucionó esta mutación y si puede aplicarse en medicina humana o en la industria.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una mutación única en pulpos de aguas poco profundas aumenta la precisión en la fabricación de proteínas, y al copiarla en bacterias se reduce a la mitad el error, abriendo nuevas formas de mejorar la producción de proteínas.
articles.image_prompt: An intricately detailed painting of a shallow-water octopus gently unfolding a glowing, delicate strand of RNA shaped like a finely woven ribbon, surrounded by softly swirling ocean currents in warm, muted blues and sandy tones, symbolizing precision and the ancient evolution of its unique protein-making mutation.

articles.title: Observation of Floquet rotational super-radiance
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10725-yA Floquet regime of rotational super-radiance has been observed in a spatiotemporally modulated ring network of resonators.
articles.content: Time-driven systems provide a framework for controlling waves through spatio-temporal modulation, which enables the synthesis of effective motion without mechanical displacement1,2,3,4,5,6,7. Within this framework, travelling-wave modulations can emulate moving media and give rise to phenomena such as Doppler-induced non-reciprocity8,9,10. A related effect is the extraction of energy from rotating media, which has been theoretically predicted to occur when waves experience sufficiently large rotational Doppler shifts11,12,13,14,15,16,17. Experimental access to this regime has remained limited due to the extreme rotation speeds required in mechanically rotating systems18,19,20,21. Here we show that Floquet-induced rotation enables access to such ultrafast rotational regimes using purely spatio-temporal modulation. When spinning at effective superluminal speeds, angular-momentum bandgaps emerge in the band structure of the underlying space–time crystal. These gaps host parametric processes that efficiently extract energy from the Floquet-rotating medium, resulting in angular-momentum-selective amplification of orbital waves within a dissipation-shaped spectral bandwidth. We realize this effect experimentally in a ring network of time-modulated resonators, where we observe a Floquet regime of rotational super-radiance mediated by non-Hermitian and parametric dynamics in space–time structured media. These results demonstrate a controllable platform for studying rotational energy transfer and angular-momentum-dependent wave amplification in space–time-modulated media.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant experimental breakthrough in controlling wave phenomena through spatio-temporal modulation, enabling ultrafast rotational regimes and energy extraction from rotating media. This represents a major advancement in wave physics and materials science with broad implications for future technologies involving wave control and energy transfer.
articles.personality_title: Scientists observe new way to boost waves using rotating energy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study how waves behave when their environment changes over time and space. This can help control waves without moving parts, using special patterns called spatio-temporal modulation.

**What happened** – Researchers created a ring of connected devices called resonators that change their properties quickly in time and space. This setup mimics very fast rotation, much faster than any mechanical spinning. They discovered a new effect called Floquet rotational super-radiance, where waves gain energy and grow stronger because of this fast, artificial rotation.

**Impact** – This is the first time this effect has been seen in an experiment. It shows a new way to control wave energy and direction by using time and space changes instead of physical motion. This could help develop new technologies that manage energy and signals in better ways, such as in communication or sensors.

**What's next step** – Scientists will explore how to use this effect in different materials and devices. They may develop new tools that use this wave amplification for practical uses like improving wireless signals or creating new types of energy transfer systems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have found a new method to amplify waves by simulating super-fast rotation through time and space changes in a ring of resonators.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques observent une nouvelle façon d’amplifier les ondes grâce à une énergie rotative
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient le comportement des ondes lorsque leur environnement change dans le temps et l’espace. Cela permet de contrôler les ondes sans pièces mobiles, en utilisant des motifs spéciaux appelés modulation spatio-temporelle.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont créé un anneau de dispositifs connectés, appelés résonateurs, qui changent rapidement leurs propriétés dans le temps et l’espace. Cette configuration imite une rotation très rapide, bien plus rapide qu’une rotation mécanique. Ils ont découvert un nouvel effet appelé super-radiance rotationnelle de Floquet, où les ondes gagnent de l’énergie et deviennent plus fortes grâce à cette rotation artificielle rapide.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que cet effet est observé en expérimentation. Cela montre une nouvelle façon de contrôler l’énergie et la direction des ondes en utilisant des changements dans le temps et l’espace plutôt que des mouvements physiques. Cela pourrait aider à créer de nouvelles technologies pour mieux gérer l’énergie et les signaux, par exemple dans les communications ou les capteurs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier comment utiliser cet effet dans différents matériaux et appareils. Ils pourraient développer de nouveaux outils qui utilisent cette amplification d’ondes pour des applications pratiques comme améliorer les signaux sans fil ou créer de nouveaux systèmes de transfert d’énergie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé une nouvelle méthode pour amplifier les ondes en simulant une rotation ultra-rapide grâce à des changements dans le temps et l’espace dans un anneau de résonateurs.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos observan nueva forma de amplificar ondas usando energía rotatoria
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian cómo se comportan las ondas cuando su entorno cambia en tiempo y espacio. Esto ayuda a controlar las ondas sin partes móviles, usando patrones especiales llamados modulación espacio-temporal.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores crearon un anillo de dispositivos conectados llamados resonadores que cambian rápidamente sus propiedades en tiempo y espacio. Este sistema imita una rotación muy rápida, mucho más rápida que cualquier giro mecánico. Descubrieron un nuevo efecto llamado superradiancia rotacional de Floquet, donde las ondas ganan energía y se vuelven más fuertes gracias a esta rotación artificial rápida.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que este efecto se observa en un experimento. Muestra una nueva forma de controlar la energía y dirección de las ondas usando cambios en tiempo y espacio en lugar de movimiento físico. Esto puede ayudar a desarrollar tecnologías nuevas para manejar mejor la energía y señales, como en comunicaciones o sensores.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos explorarán cómo usar este efecto en diferentes materiales y dispositivos. Podrían crear nuevas herramientas que usen esta amplificación de ondas para usos prácticos como mejorar señales inalámbricas o crear nuevos sistemas de transferencia de energía.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los investigadores han encontrado un nuevo método para amplificar ondas simulando una rotación ultrarrápida mediante cambios en tiempo y espacio en un anillo de resonadores.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing circular network of interconnected resonators radiating waves of light that spiral outward in smooth, rhythmic patterns, symbolizing controlled rotational energy and wave amplification within a dynamic, spinning space-time crystal.
